chapters.title: Grandma’s chicken and the author’s journey toward vegetarianism
chapters.text:
The author's relationship with food was shaped by his family, especially his grandmother. She cooked just one dish, chicken with carrots, yet as kids, Jonathan and his brother Frank thought she was the greatest cook in the world. She had survived World War II, and her view of food was nothing like ours today. Unhealthy products did not exist in her mind. She believed the rounder the kids, the healthier they were, and she even made sure they drank Coca-Cola. For her, food carried fear, faith, and love.

The way we see food is shaped by what we went through. For some, food is sacred. For others, it is easy to throw it away without a second thought.

There was a moment when the Foer brothers were eating dinner with their vegetarian babysitter. They asked why she would not eat the chicken. She said she did not want to hurt any living being. That was the moment the author realized a chicken could feel pain the way a person can. He became a vegetarian for a few years, but the resolve faded. He ended up pretending to be vegetarian at school while eating meat at home. In college, he kept eating meat because he loved the taste.

When Foer met his future wife, they had one thing in common. They both kept slipping from vegetarianism back into eating meat. After the wedding, they promised each other they would be vegetarian, and again they failed. But when their son was born, Foer saw a chance to make a change, at least for this small new person. As we grow up, our parents pass on the rules of eating. We are told not to eat soup for breakfast, an odd rule, and we are told chicken is fine but parrots are out, which would surprise a hawk. These rules shape who we become more than we realize.

Reading Eating Animals will help you see your own eating habits and your family's food culture with new eyes. You will step into the world where animals live. The journey will be eye-opening, because some facts lay bare the shameful way we raise animals for food. You will probably feel pulled toward vegetarianism and toward helping animals in other ways, which is good, because they need our support now more than ever.

chapters.title: Owning a pet shows that every animal has its own personality
chapters.text:
What about our attitudes toward animals? That, too, is passed from our parents to us and then to our children. Foer's grandmother and mother were afraid of dogs, and the author followed their lead. He panicked every time a dog ran at him in the park or in a friend's home. Naturally, he never imagined himself as someone who would adopt a dog. Then he saw George, the future puppy of his life, asleep on Seventh Avenue. At first, he could not bring himself to feed her from his hand. Soon, he was letting her lick his face. From that moment, he was a typical dog lover who melted at the sight of a wagging tail.

Anyone who has a pet has likely noticed that animals are more than just needs and instincts, more than safety and food. Animals have personalities, which brings them closer to us. Foer saw that George had her own view of the world and even human-like feelings. Given his philosophical and practical leanings toward vegetarianism, the author understood clearly that he would never eat his dog or anyone else's. So why would he eat other animals? And why do people in some places, like parts of China and South Korea, not feel guilty about eating dogs for supposed health reasons?

Food ethics are tangled, because food ties into taste buds and taste, personal stories, and social histories, in the words of Jonathan Safran Foer.

Each country has its own culture that shapes what people eat. But we are also shaped by family, surroundings, social norms, and ethics. Sadly, ethics is rarely at the top of the list.

Many well-known people changed their minds about meat and became vegetarian, like Kafka, the German-speaking writer. Shame was the word that captured his view of eating animals. Once, while visiting a Berlin aquarium, Kafka started talking to the fish. He said he could finally look at them without guilt, because he had stopped eating them.

Shame can push us toward more compassion.

When people feel shame, they realize they traded away responsibility to someone or something for a passing pleasure. Shame does not let us forget something important. In Kafka's case, fish have been a symbol of forgetting, because people tend to think less about sea creatures than about farm animals when it comes to saving lives.

chapters.title: What pushed the author to research how meat is produced
chapters.text:
Children and their direct questions wake up shame in parents by asking uncomfortable things. Adults have to find answers that will not confuse their kids or make them think their parents are bad. Children love to ask why. About meat, a child might ask, why do we eat chicken but not dogs? From a child's point of view, a chicken and a dog are not that different. They are living beings that feel pain and do not want to be eaten. How do we answer that without making ourselves look cruel? With that in mind, Foer decided to learn as much as he could, so he would not feel ashamed, and so he could raise a child who is more aware and more understanding.

To even begin explaining our culture of eating animals, the author had to draw a line between an animal and a person. It is hard to know what an animal feels, and we can only guess based on our own experience. Imagine you have a cat and you need to travel for a week. The cat has all the food and water it needs, but it is alone. Would it feel lonely? Would it suffer from your absence?

To treat animals better, we need to see them as people who look different from us.

Cruelty is the word that comes to mind when you dig into how our food reaches our plates. People invented the word bycatch to describe sea creatures trapped in fishing nets by chance. In reality, it is not an accident, because the nets are built and set in a way that pulls in far more sea life than the catch. To get one pound of trawled shrimp, twenty-six pounds of other sea creatures die. They get caught, they die, and they are tossed back into the sea. We do not even use that twenty-six pounds for food. So the author felt that when he sat down to a plate of five shrimp, he had to think about all the lives lost to bring him that small meal.

A useful term: anthropodenial describes the habit of ignoring the deep similarities in experience between animals and humans.

chapters.title: Sneaking into a farm and what it hides
chapters.text:
With an anonymous animal activist called C, Foer slipped into a turkey farm at night. At first glance, the birds looked content, warmed by the lights in their sheds. Then the author saw many dead turkeys lying near the living ones, full of scars, wounds, and signs of exhaustion. He watched C cut the throat of one dying turkey to end its pain. C had once worked in a poultry plant, doing the grim job of killing birds that the mechanical throat slitter had missed. She remembered the first time she saw the conditions the chickens lived in. The cages were too small for the birds to move, and they never got to step outside. That was when she knew that for those birds, death was a release from a life of suffering.

It all started with domestication, a shared evolution that helped humans and certain animals survive. Our ancestors did not force animals to become domestic. The wild was full of hardship, and it helped certain species to live with people, who offered care and safety in exchange for milk, eggs, and meat. At the time, animals had a good chance of living longer with humans than dying young in the wild. That bargain was fair, until modern times.

As farming grew and populations swelled, people saw a way to make more meat with less feed. For chickens, they bred bigger birds, the broilers. The bird's life shrank from years to about six weeks, which brought in more money for less cost. In 1946, people turned to genetics to shape the chickens we eat today. The birds grew sixty-five percent heavier, but they grew weaker, so farmers began to give them drugs to keep them alive.

In Foer's words, biodiversity gave way to genetic uniformity.

In the end, humans raised only two kinds of chickens: those bred for more meat and those bred for more eggs. The animals lost their individuality and became nothing more than food in the name of cheap production.

Raising animals for food is a perfect example of a profit-first system that cares more about money than about the lives of the animals.

chapters.title: The pandemic risk of factory farming
chapters.text:
Given how little care factory farms give the birds they raise, chicken health is far from great. The birds are often contaminated with bacteria like salmonella, or they are so weak that even a mild infection can take them down. Instead of fixing the conditions, farmers pump the birds full of drugs, especially antibiotics. You could understand that when a chicken is sick and needs help. But farmers give antibiotics to chickens that are not even sick yet, which is called nontherapeutic use.

What is the harm in that, you might ask? Preventing disease sounds good, like taking vitamins and drinking warm tea to ward off a cold. But trying to prevent a bacterial or viral infection with drugs can actually make things worse. Have you heard about the rise of drug-resistant bacteria and viruses? When a sick person or animal takes a pill, most germs die. But a few stronger ones survive and learn new ways to resist the drug. Next time the same illness hits, the same medicine may not work. The hard part is that scientists cannot invent new antibiotics fast enough, leaving us exposed until they find new drugs that harm the germ without harming the person.

Even if you do not take antibiotics when you are well, you can end up taking them through the meat of farmed animals. Millions of people now face the risk of a pandemic because germs keep mutating and becoming resistant to our drugs. For example, the H1N1 swine flu that spread in 2009 and 2010 started at a pig farm in North Carolina. Scientists who studied it were stunned to find that the strain had combined genes from pig, human, and bird viruses. That mix kept the pandemic going for an entire year, given how complex the germ was.

Taking antibiotics to prevent a bacterial infection is a bad idea.

chapters.title: More cruelty and a firm commitment to vegetarianism
chapters.text:
Raising cattle is also a cruel process. The cows are killed when they are only about a year old. In the past, farmers cared for their animals for four or five years. Temple Grandin, an American scientist, uncovered another grim truth about slaughterhouses. She found that one in four slaughterhouses did not kill every animal with a single blow. That means some cows suffered the pain until they finally died.

Facts like these pushed Foer to commit fully to vegetarianism. He wanted his son to eat well, so he looked into whether a vegetarian diet is healthy. It turns out that vegetarianism can be just as healthy as a diet that includes meat. Once he no longer worried about nutrition, he could cut all ties with factory farms.

Becoming a vegetarian to spare animals from cruelty may feel like too small a step. You might think that one person cannot change much by changing what they eat. Foer does not see it that way. You have more impact than you think. The American National Restaurant Association reported a rise in demand for vegetarian meals. They suggested that every food service spot add at least one vegetarian option. It turns out that a group of friends with even one vegetarian will often pick a place that serves vegetarian food.

Vegetarianism is about freedom of choice, and that ties into American history. The United States was once called the land of the free, partly because its people did not depend on Europe for food. Americans built their own farms and fed a large population. Today, we need the freedom to avoid certain foods.

Food is not just a symbol of freedom, but the first requirement of freedom, in the words of Jonathan Safran Foer.

The diet choices parents make shape the food habits of the whole family. Teaching kids about food culture from an early age helps them make clear ethical choices later. The more people give up meat, the better chance we have of ending cruel factory farming.

Vegetarianism is not dangerous for health. But if you decide to go vegetarian, it is wise to talk to a doctor who can help you avoid meat in a safe and healthy way.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Becoming a vegetarian is a hard path, because it means breaking a food culture built over centuries. Most likely, your family eats meat, and you grew up with turkey on Thanksgiving and veal at the cookout. In a way, switching to a meat-free life can feel like trying to quit smoking. Foer went back and forth between vegetarianism and meat, much like smokers who quit and then relapse many times. It is always better to try than to give up before you start. Eating Animals does not list the benefits of vegetarianism in a straight line. The author describes what the farming culture really looks like, and that alone is enough to make you at least consider plant-based food as a solid alternative to meat.

It is tough to accept how modern farmers treat birds and cattle. Knowing that this kind of cruelty sits so close to us shakes our faith in food ethics. But as Foer argues, you can do your part. With more restaurants and supermarkets offering vegetarian food, and more people going meat-free, we are clearly moving in the right direction. If this book moved you to try vegetarianism, do not be afraid of the hard parts of building new eating habits. Even if you simply cut back on the meat you usually eat, the animal world will thank you, because the more people step away from factory farming, the less chance it has to survive.

Try this. Ask your family about their food habits and views on eating. This can teach you a lot about culture and the choices people make. Try adding more vegetarian meals to your week, like salads, nuts, fruits, and seeds, and watch how you feel. You may notice more energy from the vitamins in those foods. Visit an animal shelter and ask what they need. They often need food, hay, building materials, or financial support. Pick a way you can help and give our furry and feathered friends a hand.
